content: I serve as the head of the Logistics (Jinsi) Branch. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I was ordered by the Subedar Major to assist with office security and was stationed at the main entrance. Around 10:00 AM, protesters began gathering at Narayan Gopal Chowk, Dhumbarahi, and Chundevi. Initially, it seemed like a peaceful protest, and we did not interfere. However, by 10:45 AM, we saw protesters carrying khukuris, swords, iron rods, hammers, and even SLR rifles. Construction stones piled on the Ring Road near Chundevi were used as missiles. Around 11:00 AM, the mob targeted the office with stones and petrol bombs. Government and private motorcycles, jeeps, and vans in the courtyard caught fire. Despite our use of tear gas, the sheer number of protesters allowed them to break the wire fencing and the main gate. They attacked us with sharp weapons and rods. I, along with several others, was beaten unconscious. While I was incapacitated with injuries to my head, forehead, and spine, the mob dragged ASI Milan Rai and Constable Uttam Thapa onto the street and brutally murdered them with sharp weapons. I was later informed that colleagues carried me to the roof, from where a Nepal Army helicopter rescued me and took me to the Nepal Police Hospital. Even though the police did not obstruct them that morning, the protesters intentionally targeted the office and committed murder. These individuals must face the maximum legal punishment.
84. Statement of Sub-Inspector Roshan Chand, working at Police Circle Swayambhu:

I am Sub-Inspector Roshan Chand of the Nepal Police, currently stationed at Police Circle Swayambhu since November 18, 2024, under the District Police Range Kathmandu. On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), being assigned to the Investigation Branch, I was on standby at the office. I learned about the day's events through the duty officer and social media news updates. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), police teams from this office were deployed from the morning: one under the DSP to New Baneshwor, one under Inspector Janak Dhungana to Kalanki Chowk, and another under Inspector Dinesh Thapa to the CDO Office mode. About 30–35 personnel, including branch staff under Inspector Suraj Baniya, were assigned to office security. Around 11:30 AM, following reports of vandalism and arson at VIP residences in this office’s jurisdiction, a 10-member team under a Junior Police Officer was deployed to Sitapaila Chowk. By order of the higher office, the beat police also returned to this office around 12:00 PM. At approximately 12:15 PM, a small crowd initially arrived and attacked the police with stones; at that time, police and some locals attempted to persuade them to stop. Gradually, the group of protesters grew, and the situation became more aggressive and terrifying. Some individuals were seen carrying iron rods, khukuris, sticks, stones, and bottles filled with petrol. The Inspector repeatedly used a megaphone to appeal for calm, but the protesters ignored the requests and continued their attacks. While the office and personnel were under attack, we defended the office as per the commander's legal orders using sequential force: baton charges, tear gas, rubber cartridges from shotguns, and blank fires. Every effort was made to control the continuous attacks from the mob. Protesters attacked from all four sides, breaking walls and gates; 10–15 police personnel were injured. When they breached from the south and began vandalizing and setting fire to vehicles and assets around 2:30 PM (14:30), tear gas canisters were running out. Realizing the crowd could not be contained without live rounds, but following the Inspector's order that 'the office is not greater than human life' and to avoid human casualties, we decided to secure the weapons and important materials and move to a safe location. Since we could not exit the gate with the one-ton van containing weapons and case files, we carried as many weapons as possible and moved to the nearby Chhauni Military Hospital. That day, our team only used tear gas, rubber cartridges, and blank fires as per legal orders; no protesters were injured or killed by our actions. Police personnel were injured in the attack and are still undergoing treatment. The building, vehicles, weapons, case files, and personal belongings of the staff were completely destroyed by the protesters' vandalism and arson.
85. Statement of Sub-Inspector Dinesh Raut, working at Police Circle Maligaun:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), being assigned to the Investigation Branch, I was on standby at the office. I learned about the day's events through the duty officer and social media news updates. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), a team was deployed to Baluwatar under the DSP and to Baneshwor Chowk under Inspector Dawa Tenji Sherpa, while about 20–25 personnel remained for office security under Inspector Ajay Jung Thapa. Around 11:30 AM, after receiving reports of attacks on police beats and VIP houses in our area, beat police were recalled to the office by 12:00 PM by order of the higher office. Around 1:15 PM, the Circle Head also returned to the office for security. Shortly after, a crowd of 200–300 people arrived, which gradually grew more aggressive and terrifying. Some carried iron rods, khukuris, sticks, stones, and petrol bombs, and began pelting stones at the police. Commanders formed a cordon on both sides of the road and used megaphones to appeal to the protesters, but they ignored the requests and continued attacking. As the office and personnel were attacked, we followed the commander's legal orders, using sequential persuasion, baton charges, tear gas, rubber cartridges, and blank fires to disperse the mob 3–4 times. No damage occurred at this office. Our team used only tear gas, rubber cartridges, and blank fires as per legal orders; no protesters were injured or killed.
86. Statement of Assistant Sub-Inspector Rajan Karki, working at Police Circle Gaushala:

I am Assistant Sub-Inspector Rajan Karki. On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was on regular duty at the office. On the 24th, I was on office security duty under Inspector Uddhav Pokharel. From early morning on the 24th, all personnel were at their assigned posts. Around 10:00 AM, Gen-Z protesters began appearing at Chabahil Chowk, Gaushala Chowk, and Runche Danda. They were chanting slogans and making noise. Initially, we thought it was a peaceful protest, and the police did not interfere. Around 11:00 AM, about 6,000–7,000 protesters targeted the office with khukuris, iron rods, slingshots, stones, and petrol bombs. Police protected themselves and the office from the missiles. The protesters intensified the use of petrol bombs, setting fire to government and private motorcycles, jeeps, and vans in the courtyard.
86. Original copy of the statement, Page-1, under Serial No. 85 of Schedule-5.

Although the police used tear gas to push them back, the protesters did not retreat due to their large numbers and continued attacking with stones and weapons. Subsequently, the Gen-Z protesters broke the wire fences, the main gate, and the back wall, entering the premises. They set fire to and vandalized vehicles and the building, looted 13 police weapons and ammunition, and destroyed case files and furniture. When it became clear we could not control the mob, we broke through the back wall and moved toward Pashupati Temple. Feeling unsafe there, we eventually entered the security perimeter of the Nepal Army at the Airport in Sinamangal. I and five others were significantly injured, while others sustained minor injuries. On the 24th, despite no obstruction from the police, the protesters targeted the office, committed arson, and injured personnel; they should face maximum legal action.
87. Statement of Assistant Sub-Inspector Pramod Kumar Yadav, working at the Federal Parliament Security Base, Baneshwor:

The Federal Parliament Security Base is under the command of an Inspector. There are high-point posts around the building where duty is rotated 24 hours a day with INSAS rifles. This is a prohibited area where protests are not allowed. Additional security from the Nepal Police and Armed Police Force is deployed during sessions or protests. On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), there was a Gen-Z protest for national interest and governance, and a National Assembly meeting was also scheduled. Accordingly, additional security was deployed.

I was at the south VIP gate in full gear, unarmed; teams from the Nepal Police and APF were also there. Around 11:00 AM, thousands of protesters breached the prohibited area, surrounded the building, and attempted to enter by breaking walls and gates. Security personnel tried to persuade them, but they became anarchic and continued their attempts. They attacked the high posts with stones and vandalized gates and police posts. The mob turned into a riot and began committing arson. Security forces had batons, gas guns, INSAS, and SLRs. As the riot escalated, the APF used gas guns and later blank fires when the situation wasn't controlled. This continued periodically throughout the day. Many police were injured by stones. In the evening, after the crowd was contained, I learned through Facebook and social media about injuries and deaths among protesters. An Army team also arrived for security. On the morning of the 24th, the Army, APF, and Nepal Police were securing the building. Protests by the Gen-Z group continued from all sides. Around 1:00 PM (13:00), an overwhelming number of protesters gathered, targeting the building with stones, slingshots, and petrol bombs. They used tools like pickaxes to break the perimeter walls and entered with khukuris and spears, eventually setting the Parliament building on fire. We then secured our weapons and ammunition. On the 24th, the Nepal Army did not play any protective role as the protesters burned the building.
88. Statement of Assistant Sub-Inspector Pradip Singh Dhami, working at the Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari, Kathmandu:

I work in the Logistics (Management) Branch at the Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari. During the Gen-Z protests on September 8 and 9, 2025 (Bhadra 23 and 24), I was not deployed in the field. This office primarily commands Kathmandu, Bhaktapur, and Lalitpur and issues directions. Until September 8, DIG Om Bahadur Rana was the acting chief.

I learned that then-AIG Dan Bahadur Karki (current IGP) was to be deployed from the 24th. Personnel at this office are usually busy with internal administrative work rather than field duty. Following the casualties on the 23rd, protesters were aggressively vandalizing and burning sites from the morning of the 24th. Reports arrived of police offices being attacked and weapons being looted. Reinforcements from other offices were impossible. Office-based staff then organized local security. Arson was occurring at VIP residences, police offices, Singhadurbar, and Parliament. I asked then-SSP (now DIG) Shekhar Khanal about managing the weapons in the logistics branch; he told me to report to acting chief DIG Om Bahadur Rana and not to panic. Around 2:00–3:00 PM, a large group armed with sticks, spears, pickaxes, shovels, slingshots, and khukuris attacked the office. There was a dual challenge of protecting the office and weapons while avoiding casualties among the protesters. We tried to persuade them, but they entered the premises, creating an environment where no one could be heard. I am unaware of what specific commands were issued. Personnel began getting injured: Senior Head Constable Amrit Kumar Shrestha suffered a leg injury, and Head Constable Ram Krishna Dhungana’s leg was broken. Personnel then prioritized their own safety. No one had the chance to focus on the building or weaponry. Protesters took that opportunity to set fire to vehicles and the building and took away weapons. The following weapons have not been recovered yet.
89. Statement of Police Assistant Sub-Inspector Hareram Kumal, serving at Police Division Koteshwar:

On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, during demonstrations by the Gen-Z youth generation in Kathmandu and various parts of the country, I was on standby duty at the office on the 23rd. On the 24th, I was deployed as duty officer at Police Division Koteshwar. At approximately 10:30 AM that day while I was in the duty officer's room, a group of 400–500 people came and attempted to attack the office and us. Locals from Koteshwar Chowk joined hands and persuaded the crowd that they should not set fire to the police office or beat police, and sent them toward Tinkune Baneswhar. After that, at around 11:00 AM, a mob coming from the Koteshwar overpass went to the Koteshwar Traffic Office, vandalized it, and set it on fire. Shortly after, that mob came to the gate of Police Division Koteshwar, threw stones, and hurled petrol bombs from outside, which we extinguished twice. At around 12:30, a mob of approximately 3,000–4,000 came to the gate outside Police Division Koteshwar, threw stones, and threatened from outside: 'Either come out of the office or we will burn you alive inside.' After they began attacking, we were forced to flee. As the crowd attacked from all four sides simultaneously and resistance and self-defense became impossible, police colleagues fled in all directions to save their lives. I too fled, entered a lane to the north of Police Division Koteshwar, entered a local resident's home, and hid in a small room like a storage room. During the attack by the agitators that day, some parts of my body were struck by stones, batons, bricks, kicks, and punches, but I was not seriously injured. My health condition is currently normal. I do not recognize any of the people who were attacking at that time. The statement I have given to the best of my knowledge is true and accurate.
90. Statement of Police Assistant Sub-Inspector Khemraj Adhikari, serving at Police Circle Kirtipur, Kathmandu:

I have been serving at Police Circle Kirtipur since 2081/12/06. On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was doing regular work at the Crime Investigation Branch of my own office. Even while working at the branch, I was aware of the immediate situation of the movement, and for that reason I was also troubled thinking about the next day's events and the state of peace, security, and order. I could not sleep that night because of those thoughts. Despite not sleeping until late at night, on the 24th I got up early in the morning, completed my daily routine, gathered information about the current situation, received crowd control equipment and weapons, took orders from my then-commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan, and was processing case work related to persons detained in custody on drug-related offenses on the 23rd. At around 11:00 AM, a group of approximately 1,000–1,200 demonstrators came and attempted to stone-throw at the office, whereupon the then-commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan persuaded the group of agitators and sent them away. The situation was normal for a while, but information was also received that arson and vandalism had occurred at various locations in Kirtipur. At around 12:00 noon, a group of 3,000–4,000 agitators again came, entered the office, and attempted stone-throwing, arson, and looting. Under the orders of Commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan, persuasion was attempted and graduated crowd control measures were adopted as much as possible to the point where shooting would have been required, but as per supervisory office orders and considering the immediate situation of minimizing human casualties  since firing would cause excessive human casualties  we were deployed to protect the office and its materials to the extent possible. At that point, a group of aggressive, lawless anarchists entered the office compound from all four sides and completely damaged the office and police barracks through vandalism, looting, and arson. While we were still requesting them not to commit arson, we took our weapons and went to a safe location. The entire day passed in a state of fear and terror. Even in such circumstances, due to the skilled leadership and situational assessment ability of Commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan, no human casualties occurred, and as a result the days following were easier for daily police operations and there was a positive effect on police morale. This statement is true and accurate.
91. Statement of Police Assistant Sub-Inspector Birendra Singh Bista, serving at Nepal Police Indefinite Battalion, Dillibazar:

During the movement by the Generation Z group on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, on Bhadra 24, 2082, at approximately 16:30, a group of 400–500 people attacked the Dillibazar Prison, broke the main gate of Nepal Police Indefinite Battalion Dillibazar, entered inside, committed arson at the line barracks, and entered the DIA room, vandalizing and damaging communication equipment, CCTV cameras, and monitors. Even after police made public address announcements and persuaded them, they did not comply. After using necessary force to send that group out of the prison perimeter, members of that same group set fire to buses outside and again committed arson at the line barracks. At around 20:30, after pushing the agitators back, we were successful in keeping 669 prisoners of various categories safely inside the prison. On the morning of Bhadra 25, 2082, at approximately 8:30 AM, prisoners inside Dillibazar Prison  having learned that prisoners in other prisons had been released  demanded that they too should be released and began protests and slogan-chanting from 8:30 AM. Immediately reporting to the supervisory Nepal Police Deval and Kathmandu Valley Police Victor Control and requesting additional support, the prisoners were addressed via public address. In the course of this, when prisoners attempted to break the inner main gate and exit, the team under the command of Nepal Police Indefinite Battalion Dillibazar's Police Durga Rai and a Nepali Army team successfully persuaded all prisoners to return inside the prison. Thereafter, after prisoners committed vandalism and arson inside the prison and attempted to escape from all four sides, the deployed team immediately transferred 458 prisoners to the Central Prison, and 192 prisoners including the head warden were managed to remain at Dillibazar Prison. Of these, 20 prisoners in various cases escaped and are at large  10 have since been brought back and 10 remain fugitives and are being searched for by their respective supervisory offices. This is true and accurate.
92. Statement of Police Assistant Sub-Inspector Prakash Ray, serving at Police Circle Lainchaur:

During demonstrations by the Gen-Z youth generation in Kathmandu and various parts of the country on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, on Bhadra 23 I was deployed at 8:30 AM under the command of Police Circle Lainchaur Office Chief Deputy Superintendent of Police Jageshwar Bhandari with a team of 15 personnel in the northern lane of Alpha Beta in Baneswhar, and on Bhadra 24 I was deployed from 9:00 AM in cordon duty in front of Ascol Campus in Lainchaur.

On Bhadra 23, I was deployed at 8:30 AM under the command of Police Circle Lainchaur Office Chief DSP Jageshwar Bhandari with a team of 15 personnel in the main barricade duty in the northern lane of Alpha Beta in Baneswhar. From approximately 10:00 AM, people had already begun gathering. Most of those who gathered were youth and young women in what appeared to be school and college uniforms. They were raising various slogans against the government. After that, at around 11:00 AM, a crowd of 7,000–8,000 people from Maitighar Mandala merged with the crowd already present. As the crowd reached the barricade and numbers increased, it became increasingly agitated and aggressive. The crowd chanted slogans for some time, and after a while began pushing, kicking, and targeting the barricade. My team was deployed in the front line of the barricade and we made tireless efforts for a long time to keep the barricade from collapsing. During this, our commander made announcements for a long time requesting peaceful demonstration, but the crowd's agitation and aggression did not stop. After holding the barricade for approximately 40 minutes to one hour, the force of the immense crowd collapsed the barricade toward us. Even after the barricade fell, my team and I tried to stop the crowd by forming a barricade with our batons. Despite tireless efforts, the crowd advanced toward the Parliament Building and my team was falling back. While falling back, the crowd became further aggressive toward us and began hurling stones, bricks, water bottles, and helmets at us. Even when Nepal Police's water cannon sprayed water at the crowd, the crowd did not retreat. My team, protecting itself with shields, took duty toward Gate No. 2 of the Federal Parliament Building. Even after taking duty toward Gate No. 2 of the Federal Parliament Building, stones, bricks, water bottles, and helmets continued to be hurled at us. By this time, no sound of gunfire was heard from anywhere. After approximately one hour of continuous projectile attacks, as our numbers were small, we moved inside from Gate No. 2 of the Federal Parliament Building. After we went inside, Gate No. 2 of the Federal Parliament Building was fully locked with an iron chain. Some time later, an enraged crowd made a hole in the wall on the side of the Citizen Investment Fund gate and approximately 8–10 demonstrators entered, whereupon my team took up baton charge positions and chased those who had entered back out.

Some time later, the crowd grew more aggressive and set fire to a single-story building near Gate No. 2. After the arson, my commander's team fired several rounds of tear gas toward the crowd, used sand to partially suppress the fire, then went back outside the gate and dispersed the crowd. For quite some time, the crowd kept gathering in front of the gate and we kept dispersing them. After clearing Gate No. 2, my commander's team reached Baneswhar Chowk. At an approximate time that I can't be certain of  it must have been around 2:30  I saw a group of approximately 20–30 people on motorcycles making noise, revving their engines loudly, arriving from Sankhamul toward Baneswhar Chowk. After that, for some time demonstrators kept gathering targeting the Parliament Building and in response we kept dispersing them with tear gas. During this, my commander's team expended 14 rounds of gas shells and 3 rounds of rubber bullets; we did not fire live rounds. During this, at approximately 4:00 PM I heard the sound of gunfire, but could not determine from which direction, how many rounds were fired, or how many demonstrators were injured or killed. In the course of this, my commander and I along with 3 others were injured. This continued until approximately 10:00 PM that night. After that, at approximately 11:00 PM we received primary treatment at Everest Hospital and returned to the office. Upon returning to the office that night, I had no information about any students being shot and killed.

On Bhadra 24, a team of 4 under my command was deployed from 9:00 AM in cordon duty in front of Ascol Campus in Lainchaur. At the location where I was deployed, at Golkhu Pakha Chowk, some demonstrators had gathered and were burning tires, and my team extinguished those burning tires. After that, vehicle movement at my deployed location was very minimal and people's movement was also not particularly significant. At approximately 11:00 AM, when an order came through my communication set to return to the office for office security, my team returned to the office. By the time we returned, the office had not been attacked, but at Sohrakhuttey Chowk, approximately 1,500 people were raising slogans. For office security, a team of approximately 25 personnel had already been deployed from the morning under the command of DSP Jageshwar Bhandari.

At approximately 11:20 AM, approximately 1,500 demonstrators  enraged and chanting slogans  advanced toward the office and pelted our team and the office building with bricks, stones, water bottles, and petrol bottles. Our commander made repeated public address announcements requesting the crowd to remain calm and not throw stones, but demonstrators disregarded this and continued attacking us. Even when some locals also urged the crowd to remain calm, they were similarly disregarded. As demonstrators grew more aggressive, their numbers increased. At approximately 13:55, approximately 2,500–3,000 demonstrators vandalized the mobile vehicle in front of the office gate, pushed it to in front of the old people's home, and set it on fire. After that, they vandalized the office gate, entered the office compound, and also set fire to parked vehicles and motorcycles. When the smoke from the fire began suffocating detainees in the detention cell and they began making noise and causing disturbances, we safely let the detainees out, telling them to remain in contact. After that, with the help of locals, we extinguished the fires on the vehicles and motorcycles that had been set on fire inside the office compound, preventing further damage. We sent the demonstrators inside the office compound toward Sohrakhuttey Chowk with the help of locals. As a result, the office, weapons inside the office, ammunition, communication sets, case files, and all other equipment remained safe. We never abandoned the office at any point. The above statement is true and accurate.
93. Statement of Police Senior Constable Omlal Pande, serving at Police Circle Baudha:

I had been working at the Supply Branch for the past 3 years. On Bhadra 24, 2082, from approximately 8:45 AM, demonstrators gathered from both sides of Police Circle Baudha (Tusal Chowk and Baudha Gate) and came up to the front of the Police Circle, throwing stones and raising slogans. At that time, I was distributing weapons, ammunition, and equipment to police constables while continuing my regular duties at the Supply Branch. As the number of demonstrators suddenly grew, from approximately 10:15 AM demonstrators began throwing water bottles at police constables on duty  up until then, police had maintained restraint. At approximately 10:35 AM, the number of demonstrators grew and confrontation began to occur. Among the demonstrators, some individuals were persuading the demonstrators themselves to remain calm. The manpower at the office  including the team deployed to New Baneswhar as per the security plan of the supervisory District Police Range Kathmandu  returned to the office at approximately 10:45 AM, and weapons and equipment were being distributed to that team as well incrementally. As the number of demonstrators continued to grow, at approximately 11:35 AM, numbers coming from Jorpati and Tusal Chowk direction merged with those groups, and as they gathered together barricades and street furniture outside the office to burn them in the middle of the road, at approximately 12:25 PM tear gas was fired by police for the first time. Among those who came to attack, some were of the mindset that the office should not be damaged, while a large number of personnel were presenting themselves in an anarchic manner. The number of demonstrators reached approximately 15,000–20,000. From the demonstrators' side, all furniture, computers, and all electronic equipment, CCTV cameras, servers, monitors, and all other materials in the office were damaged. Among the weapons: (1) .303 Rifle, barrel serial nos. (300485 and 22798 burned  since recovered), (24131 looted)  total 3 barrels damaged; related ammunition (live 1909 rounds and blank 29 rounds looted); (2) SLR live rounds 2137 and blank rounds 59 looted; shotgun rubber cartridges 194, buckshot pellets 385 rounds looted; (3) T-54 pistol, 1 barrel (55011381) looted  since recovered; related rounds 172 looted; (4) Multi gas gun barrel 11006163, 1 barrel looted  since found in burned condition; (5) SMG ammunition 22 rounds looted; (6) CF 98 pistol ammunition 148 rounds; (7) Hornet rifle ammunition 975 rounds; (8) Magnum rifle ammunition 199 rounds; (9) .38 bore revolver, 11 barrels looted  2 barrels (serial nos. 4716 and 3638 since recovered), 9 barrels still missing; (10) T-77 pistol ammunition 12 rounds looted. No use of weapons by demonstrators was known; no information available on casualties from demonstrators' side; 8–9 police personnel were injured and no one died. I respectfully wish to state this.
94. Statement of Police Senior Constable Dipesh Lama, serving at Police Circle Maligaun:

Since I was serving as Havildar Major on Bhadra 23, 2082, and the Gen-Z group was holding a peaceful program at Maitighar Mandala, Ward No. 11, Kathmandu Metropolitan City, Kathmandu district that day, I was on standby at my own office following the necessary security plan. I learned about the events of that day after the duty officer informed me and news came through social media. On Bhadra 24, 2082, under the command of DSP, a team went to Baluwatar; under Police Inspector Dawa Tenji Sherpa's command to Baneswhar Chowk; and under Police Inspector Ajay Jung Thapa's command for office security  branch staff and approximately 20–25 personnel in total. At approximately 11:30 AM that day, upon receiving information that police beats and VIP residences in this office's ward area had been vandalized and set on fire, per orders from the higher authority, the beat police also returned to this office at 12:00 noon. At approximately 1:15 PM, the office chief also came to the office and was deployed for office security. After that, a crowd of 2–3 hundred people gradually grew more aggressive and the situation became more aggressive and terrifying. Some individuals were carrying rods and khukuris, some came carrying batons, stones, chunks of concrete, and petrol-filled bottles and began throwing stones and attacking police. Commanders cordoned both sides of the road outside the office, making public address announcements and persistently requesting and persuading demonstrators. Disregarding those requests, demonstrators continued attacking. As police and the office came under attack, as per the legal orders of the commander  using persuasion, baton charge, tear gas, shotgun rubber cartridges, and aerial fire in graduated sequence as needed  we drove away the crowd 3–4 times, and no damage whatsoever occurred to this office. From our team, in accordance with legal orders for crowd control, only tear gas shells, shotgun rubber cartridges, and live bullets for aerial fire were used, and no demonstrators were injured and no human casualties occurred. This is true and accurate.
95. Statement of Constable Jholai Tharu Chaudhary:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was on regular duty at the office. On the 24th, I was on office security duty under the command of Police Inspector Uddhav Pokhrel. From early morning on Bhadra 24, all police personnel were deployed at their assigned duties. From around 10:00 AM, Gen-Z movement activity began from Chabahil Chowk, Gaushala Chowk, and the road at Runche Danda. Agitators were raising slogans and marching. Up until then, we considered it a peaceful movement and there were no measures from the police side to obstruct the movement  we were just observing it. From approximately 11:00 AM, 6,000–7,000 agitators, with khukuris, various iron rods, slingshots, wooden planks, bamboo sticks, and petrol bombs in hand, began targeting the police office with stone-throwing. While shielding against stones and petrol bombs hurled by the agitators, police were protecting themselves and securing the office. During this, agitators began hurling petrol bombs rapidly, which caused the government and personal motorcycles, police jeeps, and vans parked in the police office compound to catch fire. Police used tear gas to push the agitators back, but as they were in large numbers, they did not retreat and continued attacking police with stones and other improvised weapons. After that, Gen-Z agitators broke the wire fences and main entrance as well as the rear wall of the office and entered the office compound, setting fire to and vandalizing vehicles and the office building, while also looting 93 police firearms and ammunition. They vandalized and set fire to case files, other furniture, windows, and doors. Seeing that my team and I could no longer control the crowd, we broke through the rear wall of the office and headed toward Pashupatinath Temple. Not feeling safe there either, we again entered the security perimeter of the Nepali Army at the Airport in Sinamangal. In that incident, 5 of us including myself were seriously injured while others were lightly injured. On Bhadra 24, even when police did not obstruct or hinder the agitators, the agitators deliberately targeted and set fire to the police office, and threw stones and injured police on duty  the agitators must be prosecuted to the full extent of the law. This is my full statement, nothing further.
96. Statement of Police Senior Constable Ramchandra Yadav, serving at Police Circle Singha Durbar:

While demonstrations were happening in various parts of the country, on Bhadra 23 I was serving in the role of Havildar Major at Singha Durbar Police Circle. A police team was deployed for security during the movement by the Gen-Z youth generation. At approximately 2:00 PM, I was informed through the duty officer that the crowd gathered at Maitighar Mandala had broken through the restricted zone and attacked the Federal Parliament Building in Baneswhar. After that, I and my team remained at the office in a state of high alert and readiness. I learned about police bullets hitting people during the attack on the Federal Parliament Building through TikTok live on social media. That day, I and my team stayed at the office all day and no unpleasant incident occurred at the location where I was deployed.

On Bhadra 24, 2082, during the Gen-Z youth generation movement, police teams were deployed and I was assigned for office security under the command of Police Inspector Sunil Karki with a team of 25 personnel. After receiving information through media and communication sets that arson, vandalism, and looting had occurred at various police offices, per orders received through the communication set, I sent the weapons and equipment at this office to Nepal Police Headquarters Naxal, Kathmandu on a one-ton vehicle. Among the vehicles at this office, the office chief's vehicle, one-ton, and CM716's vehicle were saved; the mobile vehicle CM713, QRT, and C719, C70's motorcycles could not be saved despite efforts  they were burned and destroyed by the fire set by demonstrators. After receiving information through media and the control room that most government and police offices had been vandalized and set on fire, I kept all teams on high alert and on standby at the office. After that, approximately 4,000 demonstrators came to the gate at approximately 19:00 that night, and I took off my uniform, dressed in plainclothes, mingled with the agitators in a way that they did not know I was a police officer, and when they started setting fire to the barracks and case section of this office, I prevented the arson and saved important documents. However, the demonstrators tore and burned 27 banking crime case files in the case section. Being unable to control the thousands of agitators, the police inspector's room, administration branch, case section room no. 9, and certain CFD section rooms were burned. Even as they finished the arson and were leaving, I was still inside the office compound. Even when my own belongings box was vandalized right in front of me, I could not reveal my identity and could only persuade them. Still, I kept telling the agitators that the police office is also our property, stopped them from causing further harm, and my mind was set on saving whatever could be saved. That night, I and Sub-Constable Dinesh Chaudhary slept in the barracks. After that, the demonstrators did not return, and at approximately 2:00 AM the office chief came, inspected the damage at the office, and ordered us to stay there.
97. Statement of Police Senior Constable Kumar Raut, serving at Police Circle Lainchaur:

During demonstrations by the Gen-Z youth generation in Kathmandu and various parts of the country on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, on Bhadra 23 I was part of a team of 40 police including myself deployed for office security at Police Circle Lainchaur under the command of Police Inspector Pradeep Subedi. On that day, after the Gen-Z generation movement demonstrators began demonstrating at Maitighar Mandala in Kathmandu from early morning, the duty officer informed us of the supervisory police control's instruction for police personnel deployed for security to remain at their respective offices on high alert, and I was at the office. The Gen-Z demonstration advanced from Maitighar Mandala toward New Baneswhar Parliament Building, broke police barricades, and proceeded toward the Parliament Building. As the crowd in the demonstration became unruly, broke the Parliament Building walls, and entered the Parliament Building, the sequence of events including police having to fire guns while using graduated force  was conveyed by the duty officer and Police Inspector Pradeep Subedi to all of us deployed for security at the office, ordering us to remain on high alert. On that day, no unpleasant incident occurred at this office. On the 24th, from early morning, a police team of 30 personnel under Office Chief DSP Jageshwar Bhandari's command was deployed at the office for office security. At approximately 10:00 AM, approximately 200 demonstrators gathered at Sohrakhuttey Chowk, raising slogans against the government and burning tires as they demonstrated  our team with our commander was deployed for office security at the office gate. At approximately 11:00 AM, approximately 1,500 enraged demonstrators advancing toward the office while raising slogans pelted our team and the office building with bricks, stones, water bottles, and petrol bottles. Our commander made repeated public address announcements requesting the crowd to remain calm and not throw stones, but demonstrators disregarded this and continued attacking us. Even when some locals urged the crowd to be calm, it was similarly disregarded. Demonstrators grew more aggressive and their numbers increased. At approximately 13:55, approximately 2,500–3,000 demonstrators vandalized the mobile vehicle in front of the office gate, pushed it in front of the old people's home and set it on fire. After that, they vandalized the office gate, entered the office compound, and set fire to parked vehicles and motorcycles. When the smoke from the fire began suffocating detainees in the detention cell and they began making noise and causing disturbances, we safely let the detainees out, telling them to remain in contact. After that, with the help of locals, we extinguished the fires on the vehicles and motorcycles that had been set on fire inside the office compound, preventing further damage. We sent the demonstrators inside the office compound toward Sohrakhuttey Chowk with the help of locals, and as a result the office, weapons, ammunition, communication sets, case files, and all other equipment remained safe. We never abandoned the office at any point.
98. Statement of Police Constable Man Bahadur Chand, serving at Police Circle New Baneswhar, Kathmandu:

While demonstrations were happening in various parts of the country, on Bhadra 23 I was on picket duty at New Baneswhar Chowk and on the 24th I was on duty at the office for office security. On Bhadra 23, 2082, at approximately 9:00 AM, I along with three others took duty at New Baneswhar Chowk under the command of a Sub-Inspector. At that time, demonstrators were gathering. As the gathering grew, a crowd of thousands of demonstrators from Maitighar Mandala was raising slogans in front of New Baneswhar Chowk. Shortly, that crowd began attempting to break the barricade and throwing stones, bottles, and bricks at security personnel. At that time, commanders present there repeatedly urged them not to break the restricted zone, but the crowd disregarded those entreaties and at approximately 11:30 broke through the barricade and advanced toward the Parliament Building while attacking police. After that, there was a threat to the picket duty itself and I had no weapon with me. So along with other police teams, I also entered the Parliament Building. Even after entering the Parliament Building, the crowd's aggression did not stop. Demonstrators had improvised weapons in their hands. They were doing things like breaking and punching holes in the Parliament Building walls, climbing security posts, and so on. While doing so, the team inside the Parliament Building repeatedly chased groups of demonstrators who had entered back outside with baton charges. But after going outside, that crowd would again throw stones, bottles, and bricks at us. They also had flammable substances in bottles filled with petrol, which they hurled at the water cannon vehicle outside the gate. As the situation grew more complex, commanders used public address, persuasion, baton charges, and tear gas but the situation could not be normalized. Shortly after, the crowd even attempted to climb posts and seize weapons and communication sets. After a moment gunfire was heard, but it was not known what happened or who fired. The situation continued like this until the evening, and at approximately 21:00 PM after the demonstrators left, I along with my team returned to Police Circle Baneswhar.

On Bhadra 24, when I opened social media, I learned that people had been injured and some students had also died in the previous day's demonstration. On Bhadra 24, I was deployed from early morning for office security under the command of Police Inspector Ramesh Rai. At an uncertain time, I heard through the communication set that police offices, government structures, and politicians' homes elsewhere were being attacked. After that, the Police Inspector instructed us to remain on high alert as crowds of demonstrators could also arrive here. Shortly, a crowd of approximately 2,000–3,000 came to the office building and began throwing stones and bricks from outside. Even after we repeatedly fired tear gas and aerial rounds to stop the crowd, the agitated crowd entered the office, and the Armed Police Force unit that was nearby also left that location, so demonstrators broke through the main gate and entered the office. In the course of this, per orders not to cause human casualties, we were compelled not to use force for the safety of people's lives. As a result, the crowd of thousands of demonstrators gathered in front of the police office  taking advantage of police not using force  entered the police office, sprinkled flammable substances including kerosene, diesel, and petrol they had brought with them, hurled petrol bombs, set fire to vehicles in the office parking and the office building, completely destroying everything. After that, the police team deployed for office security was unable to stop the agitated crowd, and we went to the nearby 7.5 Niketan School for a safe location. Agitators also came to that school, forcibly made police personnel including myself remove our uniforms, lined us up, and attacked with iron rods, sharp weapons, bricks, stones, punches, and kicks  chasing and beating us  and took us 100 meters ahead to under the Bagmati River bridge with the intent to kill, physically tortured us, forcibly pushed us into the Bagmati River, and from both banks ruthlessly pelted bricks and stones, beating us mercilessly until we were near death, seriously injuring us. Several police personnel including myself were injured in that incident. I was injured in that incident and was admitted for 1 day at Civil Service Hospital in Baneswhar and 9 days at Nepal Police Hospital in Maharajgunj for treatment. In that incident, I sustained deep injuries to my head and various parts of my body. I have stitches in 3 places on my head and my right shoulder bone was cracked and I was unable to work. Demonstrators vandalized the police office and committed arson and looting of weapons, ammunition, cash, and other goods in the office. I am currently serving at Police Circle Baneswhar after recovery.
99. Statement of Police Constable Niraj Kumar Singh, serving at Police Circle Maharajgunj:

While I was handling the supply branch duties, the Subedar Major ordered me to perform office security duty, so I was stationed at the main entrance of the office. From early morning on Bhadra 24, all police personnel were deployed at their assigned duties. From approximately 10:00 AM, Gen-Z movement activity began at Narayan Gopal Chowk, Dhumbhara Chowk, and from the lane leading to Chunadevi. Agitators were raising slogans and marching. Up until then, we considered it a peaceful movement and there were no measures from the police side to obstruct the movement  we were just observing it. From approximately 10:45 AM, khukuris, swords, hammers, iron rods, wooden planks, bamboo sticks, and SLR rifles began to be seen in the hands of agitators. The ring road section in front of Police Circle Maharajgunj office was under construction, and stones for that construction had been placed along the side of the road leading to Chunadevi on the south side of that road. At approximately 11:00 AM, Gen-Z movement demonstrators suddenly began targeting police and the police office with stone-throwing and petrol bomb attacks. While shielding against stones and petrol bombs hurled by the agitators, police were protecting themselves and securing the office. During this, agitators began hurling petrol bombs rapidly, which caused the government and personal motorcycles, police jeeps, and vans parked in the police office compound to catch fire. Police used tear gas to push the agitators back, but as they were in large numbers, they did not retreat and continued attacking police with stones and other improvised weapons. After that, Gen-Z agitators broke the wire fences and main entrance of the office and entered, indiscriminately attacking police on duty with various sharp improvised weapons. On Bhadra 24, my duty was at the main entrance. The agitators beat and slashed police personnel including myself, rendering us unconscious, while also committing arson, vandalism, and looting police weapons. During this, Sub-Inspector Milan Rai and Police Constable Uttam Thapa were dragged, stomped on, and taken outside to the road where they were ruthlessly struck with sharp weapons. My head, legs, spine were struck, and the agitators' hammer broke 6 of my lower front teeth, and I sustained serious injuries to various parts of my body, falling into unconsciousness. Other police personnel were also being beaten. I later learned that some police colleagues had carried me to the roof of the office, from where a Nepali Army helicopter came and rescued me and brought me to Nepal Police Hospital. On Bhadra 24, even when police did not obstruct or hinder the agitators, the agitators deliberately targeted the police office, beat Sub-Inspector Milan Rai and Police Constable Uttam Thapa and injured them, and murdered the police on duty. Agitators who brutally murdered police on duty must be prosecuted to the full extent of the law.
99(क). Statement of Police Constable Deepak Prasad Ghimire, serving at Police Circle Janasewa:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, under the command of Office Chief C-66 of Police Circle Janasewa where I serve, a team of 11 including Police Inspector C-663 was deployed as a striking team at the Singha Durbar perimeter from 8:30 AM. At approximately 11:30 AM, as a crowd of demonstrators carrying placards with slogans was heading from Maitighar Mandala toward Baneswhar, a group arriving on bullet motorcycles carrying Nepal's flag and raising slogans suddenly attempted to enter toward the western and southern gates of Singha Durbar. A barricade was erected in front of the Health Ministry and I was deployed with an armed team in the second row, with a gas gun and 30 shells, to prevent the crowd from going toward Singha Durbar while supporting the barricade. Afterward, with additional support from Singha Durbar Circle and Armed Police Force coordinated through Victor Control, while providing support, after demonstrators appeared aggressive in Baneswhar, a curfew order was issued and everyone was informed and requested to return to their homes  information was received through Victor 007 at approximately 12:30 PM. After receiving further orders through Victor Control to go to Baneswhar as additional support, we set off via the Anamangal corridor and steel bridge route to go to Baneswhar, but as the crowd was demonstrating at that location, it was impossible to proceed. Returning and heading via Thapagaun, upon reaching the AT area, the Army team had already taken duty and the situation had become somewhat normal and demonstrators had already dispersed. C-66 briefed that demonstrations might continue on Bhadra 24 as well, so all personnel should be kept in crowd control equipment ready for immediate deployment; office security should be strengthened; unnecessary crowds gathering for demonstrations and processions should be persuaded and dispersed and not allowed to gather; and with crowd control equipment, personnel should be deployed for office and area security. Accordingly, from 7:30 AM on Bhadra 24, I was deployed for Police Circle Janasewa office security under C-66's command. While we were deployed there, from 9:00 to 10:00 AM, approximately 1,000–2,000 demonstrators were demonstrating with placards around the Juddha Statue in front of the office. Meanwhile at Siddhicharyan Chowk, approximately 7–8 individuals on bullet motorcycles wearing T-shirts with 'TOB' and 'We are Nepal' written on them entered the crowd, engaged in unruly activities, came toward the Juddha Statue, threw stones at the police office, made petrol bombs in plastic bottles with liquid substances and hurled them, and also threw stones from surrounding commercial establishments and from the office walls  while the police team under the DSP's command including myself was securing the office, protecting weapons and ammunition inside, and protecting detainees in custody. By that time, information came that police units in many Kathmandu locations had been set on fire and office weapons looted, and police personnel had also been killed. While stone-throwing from outside this police office was continuing and we were containing it, gradually unruly individuals in the demonstration carrying rods, khukuris, and batons approached saying the police office must also be set on fire, lighting fires in front of the office gate, setting fire to various public properties outside the police office compound while demonstrating and throwing stones and damaging vehicles in the police office  in that situation, Kathmandu Valley Police Office (Police Control) radio sets also broadcast instructions to save the office as much as possible, contain it, not use force, persuade, not fire guns, prepare a list of detainees and release them, save ammunition, communication sets, and weapons, and keep police personnel safe. Accordingly, with approximately 5,000 demonstrators gathered around Police Circle Janasewa, lighting fires, raising slogans, with some unruly individuals throwing petrol bombs and throwing stones  while persuading and protecting to the extent possible  at approximately 16:30, some demonstrators in the demonstration formed a mob carrying rods, khukuris, and various improvised weapons, broke and set fire to the Bishal Bazar Company gate through which Police Circle Janasewa is entered, released detainees from the office custody, and began arson, vandalism, and looting inside the office compound. Police Circle Janasewa police personnel placed weapons, ammunition, and communication equipment in bags and sacks, and Nepali Army from Vasantapur Durbar came to our office, covered us from all four sides, and took us to the Nepali Army barracks. After that, demonstrators set fire to four vehicles, the office motorcycles, and personal vehicles of police personnel at Police Circle Janasewa  they were burned and destroyed  and police personnel were hit with stones and sustained minor injuries. At around 5 PM, demonstrators completely set fire to the police office where I serve.
99(ख). Statement of Police Constable Deepak Prasad Ghimire, serving at Police Circle Janasewa:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, under the command of Office Chief C-66 of Police Circle Janasewa where I serve, a team of 11 including Police Inspector C-663 was deployed as a striking team at the Singha Durbar perimeter from 8:30 AM. I was carrying 1 megaphone and 2 handcuffs. At approximately 11:30 AM, as a crowd of demonstrators carrying placards was heading from Maitighar Mandala toward Baneswhar with slogans, a group arriving on bullet motorcycles carrying Nepal's flag and raising slogans suddenly attempted to enter toward the western and southern gates of Singha Durbar. A barricade was erected in front of the Health Ministry and I was deployed in the front row with a microphone and baton-shield team to prevent the crowd from going toward Singha Durbar while supporting the barricade. Demonstrators at Maitighar Mandala walked via Tripureshwar through Sundhara toward the National Trauma Center. As that crowd came toward Bir Hospital-Trauma Center, they threw stones at Nepal Telecom in Sundhara, breaking some windows; set fire to 2 public motorcycles on the road; also set fire to the Sundhara Police Beat; and moved toward Newroad Trauma Center. After receiving information through Victor 007 that the crowd had advanced toward the Newroad Trauma Center, I went with my team under C-66's command to Newroad, where the crowd was already dispersing and the situation had normalized. During the Gen-Z youth demonstration on Bhadra 23, 2082, the injured were admitted to the National Trauma Center for treatment, and 8 bodies of those who died during treatment were also kept at the National Trauma Center. Relatives and well-wishers who came to visit the deceased and injured vented their anger at and attempted to create disturbances with police deployed for security, resulting in further security challenges on the 24th. As demonstrations continued, beats, CRV, and QRT units under command were merged, their vehicles brought to Police Circle Janasewa for safe keeping, and from early morning on the 24th, all active police personnel were kept on full-gear standby for office security; a large team was formed; security guards at banks and gold and silver shops were alerted; patrolling was conducted in nearby areas; gold and silver traders were requested not to open shops; and a strong team was dispatched for the security of the injured at the Trauma Center. C-66 briefed that demonstrations might continue on Bhadra 24 as well, so all personnel should be kept in crowd control equipment ready for immediate deployment, office security should be strengthened, unnecessary crowds gathering for demonstrations and processions should be persuaded and dispersed and not allowed to gather, and with crowd control equipment, personnel should be deployed for office and area security. Accordingly, from 7:30 AM on Bhadra 24, I was deployed under C-66's command for Police Circle Janasewa office security. While we were deployed there, from 9:00 to 10:00 AM, approximately 1,000–2,000 demonstrators were demonstrating with placards around the Juddha Statue in front of the office. Meanwhile at Siddhicharyan Chowk, approximately 7–8 individuals on bullet motorcycles wearing T-shirts with 'TOB' and 'We are Nepal' written on them entered the crowd, engaged in unruly activities, came toward the Juddha Statue, threw stones at the police office, made petrol bombs in plastic bottles and hurled them, and also threw stones from surrounding commercial establishments and from the office walls  while the police team under the DSP's command including myself was securing the office, protecting weapons and ammunition inside, and protecting detainees in custody. By that time, information came that police units in many Kathmandu locations had been set on fire and office weapons looted, and police personnel had also been killed. While stone-throwing from outside this police office was continuing and we were containing it, gradually unruly individuals in the demonstration carrying rods, khukuris, and batons approached saying the police office must also be set on fire, lighting fires in front of the office gate, setting fire to various public properties outside the police office compound, and throwing stones and damaging vehicles in the police office. In that situation, Kathmandu Valley Police Office (Police Control) radio sets broadcast instructions to save the office as much as possible, contain it, not use force, persuade, not fire guns, prepare a list of detainees and release them, save ammunition, communication sets, and weapons, and keep police personnel safe. Accordingly, with approximately 5,000 demonstrators gathered around Police Circle Janasewa, lighting fires, raising slogans, with some unruly individuals throwing petrol bombs and throwing stones  while persuading and protecting to the extent possible  at approximately 16:30, some demonstrators in the demonstration formed a mob carrying rods, khukuris, and various improvised weapons, broke and set fire to the Bishal Bazar Company gate, released detainees from the office custody, and began arson, vandalism, and looting inside the office compound. Police Circle Janasewa police personnel placed weapons, ammunition, and communication equipment in bags and sacks, and Nepali Army from Vasantapur Durbar came to our office, covered us from all four sides, and took us to the Nepali Army barracks. After that, demonstrators set fire to four vehicles, the office motorcycles, and personal vehicles of police personnel at Police Circle Janasewa  they were burned and destroyed  and police personnel were hit with stones and sustained minor injuries. At around 5 PM, demonstrators completely set fire to the police office where I serve.
100. Statement of Police Assistant Constable Laxman Raj Jaisi, serving at Police Circle New Baneswhar, Kathmandu:

While demonstrations were happening in various parts of the country, on Bhadra 23 and 24 I was on duty at the office for office security. On both days, demonstrators had thrown stones at the office. On Bhadra 24, 2082, from 7:00 AM, 25–30 police personnel including myself were deployed for office security under the command of Police Inspector Ramesh Rai. In the morning, demonstrators numbering 1,000–1,200 repeatedly threw stones at the office, and in the afternoon a group of 4,000–5,000 demonstrators came carrying looted weapons from elsewhere, plastic bottles filled with kerosene, diesel, and petrol and other flammable substances, sharp weapons, iron rods, bricks, stones, and batons, and threw stones and bricks from outside at the Police Circle New Baneswhar office building where I was deployed. Even after we repeatedly fired tear gas and aerial rounds to stop the crowd, the agitated crowd entered the office, and the Armed Police Force unit nearby also left that location, so demonstrators broke through the main gate and entered the office. Per orders not to cause human casualties, we were compelled not to use force for the safety of people's lives. As a result, demonstrators  taking advantage of police not using force  entered the police office, sprinkled flammable substances including kerosene, diesel, and petrol, hurled petrol bombs, set fire to vehicles in the office parking and the office building, completely destroying everything. After that, the police team deployed for office security was unable to stop the agitated crowd, and we went to the nearby 7.5 Niketan School for a safe location. Agitators also came to that school, forcibly made police personnel including myself remove our uniforms, lined us up, and attacked with iron rods, sharp weapons, bricks, stones, punches, and kicks  chasing and beating us  and took us 100 meters ahead to under the Bagmati River bridge with the intent to kill, physically tortured us, forcibly pushed us into the Bagmati River, and from both banks ruthlessly pelted bricks and stones, beating us mercilessly until we were near death, seriously injuring us. Several police personnel including myself were injured in that incident. I was injured in that incident and was admitted for 1 day at Civil Service Hospital in Baneswhar and 6 days at Nepal Police Hospital in Maharajgunj for treatment. In that incident, I sustained deep injuries to my head and various parts of my body. Demonstrators vandalized the police office and committed arson and looting of weapons, ammunition, cash, and other goods in the office. The above statement is true and accurate.
